summary: Nigerian author known for novels Americanah, Half of a Yellow Sun, and Purple Hibiscus. Also wrote the short story collection The Thing Around Your Neck and the essays/talks 'We Should All Be Feminists' and 'The Danger of a Single Story.' Her epistolary book Dear Ijeawele offers feminist parenting advice. Her most recent novel is Dream Count. Public feud with Akwaeke Emezi over transphobic comments and withdrawal of endorsement of Freshwater.

works.name: Americanah (2013)
works.description: Adichie's best-known novel, about the Nigerian couple Ifemelu and Obinze. Ifemelu immigrates to the U.S., studies at Princeton on a fellowship, and writes the blog 'Raceteenth or Various Observations About American Blacks (Those Formerly Known as Negroes) by a Non-American Black.' Obinze goes to London and works illegally cleaning toilets, tries to arrange a green card marriage with Cleotilde, and is eventually deported. They reunite in Lagos after Ifemelu shuts down her blog and returns to Nigeria. Wikipedia

works.name: Half of a Yellow Sun (2006)
works.description: Novel set during the Nigerian Civil War (Biafran War). Centers on twins Olanna and Kainene, the intellectual Odenigbo, the Englishman Richard Churchill, and the houseboy Ugwu. The title references the flag of Biafra. Ugwu writes the book-within-a-book 'The World Was Silent When We Died.' Kainene disappears after crossing enemy lines. Wikipedia

works.name: Purple Hibiscus (2003)
works.description: Adichie's debut novel. Narrated by the teenager Kambili Achike. Her father Eugene is a strict, authoritarian Catholic who abuses his family — he throws a missal at his son Jaja, breaks his wife Beatrice's figurines, and scalds his children's feet. Beatrice eventually poisons Eugene. The family finds refuge with the liberal Aunty Ifeoma in Nsukka, where Kambili meets Father Amadi. Eugene's father is considered a pagan. Wikipedia

works.name: We Should All Be Feminists (2014 essay / 2012 TED Talk)
works.description: Adapted from Adichie's TED talk, this book-length essay argues for destigmatizing feminism. Defines a feminist as 'a person who believes in the social, political, and economic equality of the sexes.' Calls masculinity 'a hard, small cage' and says gender 'prescribes how we should be rather than recognizing how we are.' Remembers scoring highest on a test to become class monitor but the teacher giving the title to a boy. Her friend Okoloma was the first to call her a feminist. Sampled by Beyonce in 'Flawless.' Wikipedia

works.name: The Danger of a Single Story (2009 TED Talk)
works.description: One of the most popular TED talks ever. Adichie discusses how single narratives about people create stereotypes. She recalls writing stories as a child with blue-eyed white characters who ate apples, and writing characters who drank ginger beer despite not knowing what it was. Cites John Lok's description of Africans as 'beasts with no houses' and Chinua Achebe's concept of a 'balance of stories.' Recounts how her college roommate assumed she couldn't use a stove, and how she herself had a 'single story' of Mexicans as 'abject immigrants.' Uses the Igbo word 'nkali.' Wikipedia

works.name: The Thing Around Your Neck (2009 short story collection)
works.description: Short story collection containing 'Cell One,' 'The American Embassy,' 'The Thing Around Your Neck,' 'Jumping Monkey Hill,' 'The Headstrong Historian,' 'A Private Experience,' and 'Birdsong,' among others. Several stories deal with Nigerian immigrants' experiences and the tension between Nigerian and Western cultures. Wikipedia

works.name: Dear Ijeawele, or A Feminist Manifesto in Fifteen Suggestions (2017)
works.description: An epistolary book-length essay adapted from an email to the mother of Chizalum. Gives 15 tips on raising a feminist daughter, including to beware 'Feminism Lite' and 'teach her to love books.' Hopes Chizalum 'will be full of opinions' from 'an informed, humane, and broad-minded place.' Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie is a Nigerian author whose works are among the most frequently asked about in quizbowl among contemporary African writers. Her three novels form the backbone of most tossups: Americanah (2013) is by far the most clued, with the blog 'Raceteenth,' the Ifemelu-Obinze romance, Obinze's green card marriage attempt with Cleotilde, and nicknames like 'Ceiling' and 'The Zed' appearing repeatedly. Half of a Yellow Sun (2006), set during the Biafran War, features the twins Olanna and Kainene, the intellectual Odenigbo, the Englishman Richard Churchill, and the houseboy Ugwu who writes 'The World Was Silent When We Died.' Purple Hibiscus (2003), her debut, centers on the teenager Kambili and her abusive Catholic father Eugene, whom his wife Beatrice eventually poisons.

Adichie's nonfiction is nearly as prominent. 'We Should All Be Feminists,' adapted from her TED talk, was famously sampled by Beyonce in 'Flawless' and is cited for its definition of feminism and its description of masculinity as 'a hard, small cage.' 'The Danger of a Single Story,' one of the most-viewed TED talks, draws on her childhood experience writing about blue-eyed characters who ate apples and drank ginger beer, and her roommate's assumption she couldn't use a stove. Dear Ijeawele offers 15 tips on raising a feminist daughter for the mother of Chizalum.

Her short story collection The Thing Around Your Neck generates several clues across individual stories: 'Jumping Monkey Hill' (writers' retreat with the sexist Edward Campbell), 'The Headstrong Historian' (Nwamgba's granddaughter Grace/Afamefuna, an extension of Things Fall Apart), 'Cell One' (brother arrested after cult members steal a red Mercedes), 'The American Embassy' (woman in line after burying son Ugonna), the title story (second-person narrator wins a visa lottery), 'A Private Experience' (two women in an abandoned shop during a riot), and 'Birdsong' (affair remembered while stuck in traffic).

Controversy clues focus on her feud with Akwaeke Emezi and withdrawal of endorsement from Freshwater, her transphobic comments, and defense of J. K. Rowling. Her most recent novel, Dream Count (2025), about four immigrant women during the Covid-19 pandemic, has begun appearing in bonuses. She also wrote the play For Love of Biafra and the short story 'The Arrangements' about Donald Trump's wife.
recursive_suggestions: Americanah — extremely frequently clued, could warrant its own topic search
Half of a Yellow Sun — very frequently clued
Akwaeke Emezi — appears in multiple Adichie clues, Freshwater separately clued
Chinua Achebe / Things Fall Apart — frequently cross-referenced with Adichie
Ben Okri / The Famished Road — appears in multiple bonus thirds alongside Adichie
